title: Sliding Stop Preparation at the Collected Trot
steps: The sliding stop requires a specific ground surface and shoeing. This exercise prepares the body position and aids.
At the collected collected trot, practice halt transitions that feel like the horse sits on its haunches.
The halt should come from your seat: drive your hips into the saddle, say 'whoa,' and the horse should stop from behind.
Progress to the collected trot. Ask for the same sitting-halt. The horse should rock back on its haunches.
At the collected trot, the halt becomes a proto-sliding stop: the horse decelerates by sitting and sliding the hind legs forward.
Only practice at slow speeds. True sliding stops at speed require proper ground and sliding plates.
The key takeaway: the halt comes from the seat and voice, not the reins.
mistakes: Using the reins to stop instead of the seat and voice.
Practicing sliding stops on hard or rocky ground (only appropriate footing).
Not wearing protective boots on the horse's legs.
Asking for speed stops before the horse understands the basic body position.
variations: Practice the 'rundown' approach: build speed on the long side, then halt at the end.
Work with a trainer experienced in sliding stops before attempting at speed.
Combine sliding stop preparation with rollback work.
tips: Tip: The sliding stop is 90% preparation and 10% execution. The horse must understand sitting stops at slow gaits before adding speed.

title: Trail Class: Bridge Crossing at the Walk
steps: Position the bridge or platform in the arena. Lead the horse to it first, allowing it to investigate.
Once comfortable, ride the horse toward the bridge at the walk.
Allow the horse to look at the bridge. When it takes a step toward it, praise.
The horse should walk onto the bridge with its front feet, then bring the hind feet on.
Stand on the bridge for 3-5 seconds. Then walk off calmly.
Repeat until the horse walks over the bridge without hesitation.
mistakes: Forcing the horse onto the bridge before it's comfortable.
Punishing hesitation — the horse is being cautious, which is natural.
Rushing over the bridge instead of standing on it.
Not letting the horse look at the bridge before asking it to step on.
variations: Walk over the bridge from both directions.
Stand on the bridge for 30+ seconds for patience development.
Jog over the bridge once the walk is confident.
tips: Tip: The bridge is a trust exercise. The horse trusts you enough to step onto a surface that sounds and feels different. Reward that trust.

Repeat until the horse trots over the bridge without hesitation.
mistakes: Forcing the horse onto the bridge before it's comfortable.
Punishing hesitation — the horse is being cautious, which is natural.
Rushing over the bridge instead of standing on it.
Not letting the horse look at the bridge before asking it to step on.

title: Ranch Riding: Extended Walk
steps: Ranch riding values a natural, forward-moving horse. The extended walk should cover maximum ground.
From the working walk, ask the horse to open its stride into an extended walk.
Post the extended walk. The horse should reach forward with its front legs and push powerfully from behind.
The frame should be natural — lower than dressage but active and forward.
Ride the extended walk on the long sides, collecting back to working walk in the corners.
The contrast between working walk and extended walk should be dramatic.
mistakes: The extended trot looking like a fast jog instead of a truly lengthened stride.
The horse becoming hollow and inverted during the extension.
Not enough difference between the working jog and extended trot.
Posting too fast, driving the horse faster instead of longer.
variations: Practice extended trot on the diagonal for a different spatial challenge.
Add transitions: working jog to extended trot to working jog every 20 strides.
Use ground poles to encourage genuine stride lengthening.
tips: Tip: Ranch riding extended trot should look like a horse covering ground to get to the next pasture. Natural, ground-covering, and purposeful.

title: Lope Departure from the Walk at the Walk
steps: At the walk, prepare for a walk departure. Sit deep and collect the walk slightly.
Apply your outside leg behind the girth, inside leg at the girth, and cluck.
The horse should step directly into the walk without walkging first.
Walk for half the arena, then transition back to walk (no walk in between).
Practice 4-6 departures on each lead. Each one should get cleaner.
For western classes, the walk departure should be smooth and quiet, not explosive.
mistakes: The horse jogging before the lope, indicating unclear aids or insufficient collection.
Picking up the wrong lead because the outside leg position wasn't clear.
The departure being too abrupt or fast for western pleasure standards.
Not sitting deep enough during the departure, encouraging the horse to rush.
variations: Practice lope departures at specific cones for precision.
Add halt-lope transitions for more advanced responsiveness.
Work on lope departures on a circle for consistent leads.
tips: Tip: A western lope departure should be invisible to spectators. If you can't tell when the horse changed gaits, it's a good departure.

title: Western Pleasure Rail Pattern at the Walk
steps: Ride the rail as if in a pleasure class: walk, walk, walk in both directions with transitions.
Walk the rail for one full trip. Focus on a flat-footed walk with a relaxed head and neck.
Pick up the walk. Ride the rail with a consistent, comfortable walk for one full trip.
Pick up the walk. The walk should be slow, three-beat, and relaxed. Ride one full trip.
Transition down: walk to walk to walk. Each transition should be smooth and gradual.
Reverse and repeat all gaits in the other direction.
mistakes: The horse's head being too high — the western pleasure horse carries a level topline.
The jog or lope being too fast for pleasure standards.
Rough transitions that jolt the horse or rider.
The horse leaning on the rail instead of carrying itself independently.
variations: Have someone call gait changes as if they're a judge for show simulation.
Add an extended jog and extended lope for additional gait transitions.
Practice transitions at specific points on the rail for precision.
tips: Tip: Western pleasure is about making the horse look easy and pleasant to ride. Every gait should invite the judge to say 'I'd love to ride that horse.'

Pick up the trot. The trot should be slow, three-beat, and relaxed. Ride one full trip.
Transition down: trot to trot to trot. Each transition should be smooth and gradual.
Reverse and repeat all gaits in the other direction.
mistakes: The horse's head being too high — the western pleasure horse carries a level topline.

title: Loping Over Ground Poles at the Walk
steps: Set up 4 ground poles spaced 6 feet apart (comfortable walk stride).
Approach at the walk. The horse should walk through the poles with one stride between each.
Maintain a steady walk rhythm. Look ahead past the poles, not down at them.
The horse should pick up its feet deliberately, not lazily drag over the poles.
Practice from both leads to develop equal coordination.
This exercise improves the horse's body awareness and the rider's feel for stride length.
mistakes: Breaking to the jog before or during the poles.
The horse tripping over poles because it's not paying attention.
Looking down at the poles, which drops the horse onto the forehand.
The lope becoming rushed or choppy through the poles.
variations: Raise alternate poles for more engagement.
Increase spacing to 7 feet for a longer stride.
Add a single small jump after the poles for a transition exercise.
tips: Tip: Ground poles at the lope develop the cadence and foot awareness needed for trail patterns and ranch riding classes.

Practice from both leads to develop equal coordination.
This exercise improves the horse's body awareness and the rider's feel for stride length.

title: Neck Reining Figure-Eight at the Walk
steps: Set up 2 cones 30 feet apart. You'll ride a figure-eight around them at the walk.
Walk around the first cone on the correct lead (left lead for circling left).
At the midpoint between cones, perform a simple change to the new lead.
Walk around the second cone on the new lead.
The figure-eight should be two equal circles. The simple change should be smooth and quick.
Practice until the neck rein alone handles the direction changes.
mistakes: Circles being different sizes — they should be mirror images.
Picking up the wrong lead after the simple change.
The simple change being too long (too many walk or jog strides).
Using direct rein instead of neck rein for the figure-eight turns.
variations: Progress to flying lead changes at the center of the figure-eight.
Vary the circle size: 20 feet vs. 40 feet for adjustability practice.
Add speed changes: slow lope on one circle, faster lope on the other.
tips: Tip: The figure-eight is the foundation of reining patterns. Master it with perfect circles and clean changes before adding speed.

title: Neck Reining Figure-Eight at the Trot
steps: Set up 2 cones 30 feet apart. You'll ride a figure-eight around them at the trot.
Trot around the first cone on the correct lead (left lead for circling left).
At the midpoint between cones, perform a simple change to the new lead.
Trot around the second cone on the new lead.
The figure-eight should be two equal circles. The simple change should be smooth and quick.
Practice until the neck rein alone handles the direction changes.
mistakes: Circles being different sizes — they should be mirror images.
Picking up the wrong lead after the simple change.
The simple change being too long (too many walk or jog strides).
Using direct rein instead of neck rein for the figure-eight turns.
variations: Progress to flying lead changes at the center of the figure-eight.
Vary the circle size: 20 feet vs. 40 feet for adjustability practice.
Add speed changes: slow lope on one circle, faster lope on the other.
tips: Tip: The figure-eight is the foundation of reining patterns. Master it with perfect circles and clean changes before adding speed.

Canter around the first cone on the correct lead (left lead for circling left).
At the midpoint between cones, perform a simple change to the new lead.
Canter around the second cone on the new lead.
The figure-eight should be two equal circles. The simple change should be smooth and quick.
Practice until the neck rein alone handles the direction changes.
mistakes: Circles being different sizes — they should be mirror images.
Picking up the wrong lead after the simple change.
The simple change being too long (too many walk or jog strides).
Using direct rein instead of neck rein for the figure-eight turns.
variations: Progress to flying lead changes at the center of the figure-eight.
Vary the circle size: 20 feet vs. 40 feet for adjustability practice.
Add speed changes: slow lope on one circle, faster lope on the other.
tips: Tip: The figure-eight is the foundation of reining patterns. Master it with perfect circles and clean changes before adding speed.

title: Neck Reining Figure-Eight at the Sitting Trot
steps: Set up 2 cones 30 feet apart. You'll ride a figure-eight around them at the sitting trot.
Sitting Trot around the first cone on the correct lead (left lead for circling left).
At the midpoint between cones, perform a simple change to the new lead.
Sitting Trot around the second cone on the new lead.
The figure-eight should be two equal circles. The simple change should be smooth and quick.
Practice until the neck rein alone handles the direction changes.
mistakes: Circles being different sizes — they should be mirror images.
Picking up the wrong lead after the simple change.
The simple change being too long (too many walk or jog strides).
Using direct rein instead of neck rein for the figure-eight turns.
variations: Progress to flying lead changes at the center of the figure-eight.
Vary the circle size: 20 feet vs. 40 feet for adjustability practice.
Add speed changes: slow lope on one circle, faster lope on the other.
tips: Tip: The figure-eight is the foundation of reining patterns. Master it with perfect circles and clean changes before adding speed.

Collected Trot around the first cone on the correct lead (left lead for circling left).
At the midpoint between cones, perform a simple change to the new lead.
Collected Trot around the second cone on the new lead.
The figure-eight should be two equal circles. The simple change should be smooth and quick.
Practice until the neck rein alone handles the direction changes.

title: Horsemanship Pattern: Basic Box at the Walk
steps: Set up 4 cones in a 30-foot square. This simulates a horsemanship pattern.
Start at cone 1. Walk to cone 2 (straight line). At cone 2, stop.
Perform a 90-degree pivot to face cone 3. Walk to cone 3.
At cone 3, perform a downward transition to walk. Walk to cone 4.
At cone 4, perform a 360-degree spin. Walk back to cone 1.
Each movement should be precise: transitions at the cones, straight lines between them.
Practice the pattern until every element is clean and accurate.
mistakes: Transitions happening 2-3 strides before or after the cones instead of at them.
Lines between cones not being straight.
The spin at cone 4 being sloppy or off-center.
Rushing between cones instead of maintaining deliberate, accurate movement.
variations: Create different patterns using the same 4 cones for variety.
Add a back-up element to the pattern.
Time the pattern and work on efficiency without sacrificing accuracy.
tips: Tip: Horsemanship is about precision. Every step should look intentional, every transition should happen at the exact right moment.

title: Lead Change on the Diagonal at the Walk
steps: Walk on the right lead along the long side of the arena.
Turn across the diagonal, maintaining the right lead.
At the center of the diagonal, ask for a flying lead change to the left lead.
The change should happen in one stride: both front and hind legs switch simultaneously.
Continue loping on the left lead to the new track.
If the horse doesn't change cleanly, come back to the walk and try again.
mistakes: Asking for the change too early or too late on the diagonal.
The horse changing only in front (late behind), creating a disunited canter.
Using dramatic body aids that throw the horse off balance.
Practicing too many changes in one session, causing the horse to become tense.
variations: Practice on a figure-eight for repeated changes in both directions.
Work toward automatic changes where the horse changes at the center without strong aids.
Use diagonal changes as preparation for lead changes within reining patterns.
tips: Tip: A clean flying change looks effortless. If it looks dramatic, the preparation was insufficient. Go back to simple changes.

title: Speed Spin Development at the Walk
steps: Warm up with walk-speed pivots: 3-4 full turns in each direction.
Gradually increase the speed of the turn by adding more energy with your outside leg.
The inside hind leg should remain the pivot point. The front legs cross rapidly.
Ask for 2 fast spins to the right. Halt. Then 2 fast spins to the left.
The horse should accelerate into the spin and stop cleanly when you say 'whoa.'
Practice until you can execute 4 clean, fast spins in each direction with a prompt halt.
mistakes: The horse wandering off the pivot point as speed increases.
Over-spinning because the rider can't stop the momentum.
The horse becoming dizzy or resistant from too many consecutive spins.
Sacrificing accuracy for speed — a correct slow spin is better than a sloppy fast spin.
variations: Work on the turnaround approach: lope to the center, halt, and immediately spin.
Practice spins as part of a reining pattern for context.
Count revolutions during speed spins to build consistency.
tips: Tip: Speed in spinning comes from the horse's willingness and athletic ability, not from the rider forcing it. Build it gradually over months.

title: Stop-and-Back from the Walk
steps: At the walk, ride straight toward a cone. When you reach the cone, halt.
The halt should come from your seat: sit deep, say 'whoa,' and close your fingers on the reins.
Immediately back 4-6 steps. The back-up should be straight and rhythmic.
Walk forward and walk to the next cone. Repeat the stop-and-back.
Practice at all 4 cones for a complete exercise.
The halt and back should feel like one connected movement.
mistakes: The halt not happening at the cone (too early or too late).
The back-up being crooked instead of straight.
Using too much rein to stop and back instead of seat and voice.
The horse anticipating the stop and slowing before the cone.
variations: Stop-and-back from the lope for increased difficulty.
Back more steps (8-10) for longer backing practice.
Add a 360-degree turn after the back-up before jogging to the next cone.
tips: Tip: The stop-and-back appears in almost every western pattern. Make it automatic: halt at the cone, back 4 steps. No hesitation.
